title: Active share, step by step
intro: Three minutes to understand what active share does — and doesn't — tell you.

steps.title: What it measures
steps.body:
Active share asks a simple question: **how different is this fund from its benchmark?**

At one extreme, a fund holding exactly the index in exactly the index weights is 0% active — an index fund in disguise. At the other, a fund sharing almost nothing with the index is near 100% active.

The gauge below shows a **closet indexer**: only 15% different, yet charging active fees.

viz.label: Active share
viz.caption: 15% active — you are paying active fees for near-index behaviour.

steps.title: The formula
steps.body:
Sum the absolute weight difference for every holding, then halve it:

```
Active share = ½ × Σ | w_fund − w_index |
```

We halve because every overweight is funded by an underweight somewhere else — each real difference is counted twice in the sum. Halving lands the score on a clean 0–100% scale.

This fund is genuinely active:

viz.label: Active share
viz.caption: 72% — real positions, real deviation from the index.

steps.title: High is not the same as good
steps.body:
> [!warn] A high active share means the manager is *taking positions* — not that the positions are **right**.

Very-active funds tend to split: some consistently beat the index, others consistently lag. Active share measures **intention and risk**, never skill. Pair it with tracking error and long-horizon, full-cycle results before you judge.

recap:
**Active share = how different, not how good.**

- ~10–20% → closet indexer (active fee, index behaviour).
- ~80%+ → highly active (big bets, unknown outcome).

Ask two separate questions: *am I paying for low active share?* and *has the active risk been rewarded over full cycles?*
